introduction.p: collected the traps. A zero gradient may hide a saddle. A degenerate Hessian may hide higher-order behavior. A Lagrange multiplier calculation may miss an endpoint. Those warnings do not weaken the tools. They tell us where each tool belongs.
This review gathers the chapter into one decision path.

subsection.title: The chapter in one picture
subsection.p: Optimization begins by asking what motions are allowed.

If a point is free to move in every direction, then an interior extremum forces df=\mathbf 0, or, in gradient notation, 
abla f=\mathbf 0. If the point must stay on a constraint g=c, then only tangent directions to the constraint are allowed. At a constrained extremum, df=\lambda\,dg, or 
abla f=\lambda
abla g.
After first-order candidates are found, the Hessian reads the second-order shape.

figure.caption: The first question is not Which formula do I use? but What motions are allowed?

subsection.p:
For a twice continuously differentiable function of two variables, the Hessian test at a critical point uses A=f_{xx}, \qquad B=f_{xy}, \qquad C=f_{yy}, and \Delta=AC-B^2.
\begin{array}{c|c} \text{Condition} \amp \text{Conclusion} \\ \hline \Delta\gt 0,\ A\gt 0 \amp \text{local minimum}\\[1mm] \Delta\gt 0,\ A\lt 0 \amp \text{local maximum}\\[1mm] \Delta\lt 0 \amp \text{saddle point}\\[1mm] \Delta=0 \amp \text{test gives no conclusion} \end{array}
The number \Delta is only reading the quadratic part. If the quadratic part is silent in some direction, the function may still be decided by third, fourth, or higher-order terms.

subsection.title: Worked review example: free critical points
subsection.p: Let f(x,y)=x^3-3x+y^2-4y. Find and classify the critical points.
First compute the gradient: f_x=3x^2-3, \qquad f_y=2y-4. Set both equal to zero: 3x^2-3=0, \qquad 2y-4=0. Thus x=\pm1, \qquad y=2. The critical points are (1,2) \qquad\text{and}\qquad (-1,2).
Now compute the second partials: f_{xx}=6x, \qquad f_{xy}=0, \qquad f_{yy}=2.
At (1,2), we have A=6,\qquad B=0,\qquad C=2. So \Delta=AC-B^2=12\gt 0, and A\gt 0. Therefore \boxed{(1,2)\text{ is a local minimum.}}
At (-1,2), we have A=-6,\qquad B=0,\qquad C=2. So \Delta=(-6)(2)-0=-12\lt 0. Therefore \boxed{(-1,2)\text{ is a saddle point.}}
The same function has both behaviors. The gradient found the silent points. The Hessian listened to the first nonzero shape near each one.

subsection.title: Worked review example: absolute extrema on a rectangle
subsection.p: Let f(x,y)=x^2+2y^2-4x-4y on the rectangle 0\le x\le 3, \qquad 0\le y\le 2. Find the absolute maximum and minimum.
The domain is closed and bounded, and f is continuous, so an absolute maximum and minimum exist.

First check the interior. The gradient is \nabla f=(2x-4,\;4y-4). Set it equal to zero: 2x-4=0, \qquad 4y-4=0. Thus x=2, \qquad y=1. This point lies inside the rectangle. Its value is f(2,1)=4+2-8-4=-6.
Now check the boundary.
On the left edge, x=0,\qquad 0\le y\le2. Then f(0,y)=2y^2-4y=2(y-1)^2-2. The minimum on this edge is -2, and the maximum is 0, reached at y=0 \qquad\text{and}\qquad y=2.
On the right edge, x=3,\qquad 0\le y\le2. Then f(3,y)=9+2y^2-12-4y=2(y-1)^2-5. The minimum on this edge is -5, and the maximum is -3.
On the bottom edge, y=0,\qquad 0\le x\le3. Then f(x,0)=x^2-4x=(x-2)^2-4. The minimum on this edge is -4, and the maximum is 0, reached at x=0.
On the top edge, y=2,\qquad 0\le x\le3. Then f(x,2)=x^2+8-4x-8=x^2-4x=(x-2)^2-4. The minimum on this edge is -4, and the maximum is 0, reached at x=0.

Compare all candidates:
\begin{array}{c|c} \text{candidate} \amp f(x,y) \\ \hline (2,1) \amp -6\\ (0,0) \amp 0\\ (0,2) \amp 0\\ \text{other boundary candidates} \amp -5,\,-4,\,-3,\,-2 \end{array}
The interior gradient found the minimum. The maximum lived on the boundary.

subsection.title: Worked review example: one smooth constraint
subsection.p: Find the maximum and minimum of p(x,y)=x+2y on the ellipse x^2+4y^2=16.

Let g(x,y)=x^2+4y^2. The constraint is g=16. The gradients are 
abla p=(1,2), \qquad 
abla g=(2x,8y). The Lagrange multiplier equation is 
abla p=\lambda
abla g. So 1=2\lambda x, \qquad 2=8\lambda y. Thus x= rac{1}{2\lambda}, \qquad y= rac{1}{4\lambda}. Substitute into the constraint: x^2+4y^2=16. This gives  rac{1}{4\lambda^2} + 4\cdot rac{1}{16\lambda^2} = 16, so  rac{1}{2\lambda^2}=16. Therefore \lambda^2= rac{1}{32}, and \lambda=\pm  rac{1}{4\sqrt2}.
When \lambda=\frac{1}{4\sqrt2}, we get x=2\sqrt2, \qquad y=\sqrt2. When \lambda=-\frac{1}{4\sqrt2}, we get x=-2\sqrt2, \qquad y=-\sqrt2.

The values are p(2\sqrt2,\sqrt2)=2\sqrt2+2\sqrt2=4\sqrt2, and p(-2\sqrt2,-\sqrt2)=-4\sqrt2. The ellipse is closed and bounded, so these are the absolute extrema: \boxed{\text{maximum }4\sqrt2\text{ at }(2\sqrt2,\sqrt2),} \boxed{\text{minimum }-4\sqrt2\text{ at }(-2\sqrt2,-\sqrt2).}

Geometrically, the level lines x+2y=c slide across the ellipse. At the largest and smallest values, the line just touches the ellipse. At those touching points, the normal direction to the line is parallel to the normal direction to the ellipse.

statement.p: Why is the gradient normal to a level curve?
What is the tangent line to f(x,y)=f(a,b) at a point where \nabla f(a,b)\ne\mathbf 0?
What is the tangent plane to F(x,y,z)=F(a,b,c) at a point where \nabla F(a,b,c)\ne\mathbf 0?
Why must an interior local maximum or minimum of a differentiable function satisfy \nabla f=\mathbf 0?
Why does \nabla f=\mathbf 0 not guarantee a maximum or minimum?
What information does the Hessian contain that the gradient does not?
In the two-variable second derivative test, what does \Delta=f_{xx}f_{yy}-f_{xy}^2 measure?
What does it mean for a Hessian to be positive definite? What local shape does that suggest at a critical point?
Why does the second derivative test require the point to be critical?
Why does the test give no conclusion when \Delta=0?

Why does a constrained extremum usually satisfy 
abla f=\lambda
abla g instead of 
abla f=\mathbf 0?
What can go wrong if \nabla g=\mathbf 0 on the constraint?
Why must endpoints and corners be checked separately?
In the differential-form notation df=\lambda\,dg, what does the equation say about allowed tangent motions?
Let f(x,y)=x^2+xy+y^2. Find the tangent line to the level curve f(x,y)=3 at (1,1).
Let F(x,y,z)=x^2+2y^2+3z^2. Find the tangent plane to F(x,y,z)=9 at (1,1,\sqrt2).
Let h(x,y)=20+4x-y-x^2-2y^2. Find all critical points and classify them.
Let f(x,y)=x^3-3x+y^2-2y. Find all critical points and classify them.
Let q(x,y)=x^2+4xy+y^2. Classify the origin using the Hessian.
Let s(x,y)=x^2+xy+2y^2. Use the Hessian to decide whether the origin is a local maximum, local minimum, or saddle point.
Let f(x,y)=x^4+y^2. Explain why the second derivative test gives no conclusion at the origin, and then classify the origin directly.
Let g(x,y)=x^4-y^2. Explain why the origin is a saddle point even though the Hessian is degenerate.
Find the absolute maximum and minimum of f(x,y)=x+y on the closed disk x^2+y^2\le 9.
Find the absolute maximum and minimum of f(x,y)=x^2+y^2-2x on the rectangle -1\le x\le3, \qquad 0\le y\le2.
Use Lagrange multipliers to find the maximum and minimum of f(x,y)=3x+4y on the circle x^2+y^2=25.
Use Lagrange multipliers to find the maximum and minimum of f(x,y)=xy on the ellipse x^2+4y^2=16.
Find the point on the plane x+y+z=6 closest to the origin.
Find the point on the sphere x^2+y^2+z^2=25 where f(x,y,z)=x+2y+2z is largest.
Find the maximum and minimum of f(x,y,z)=z on the curve where x^2+y^2+z^2=9, \qquad x+y=0.

Let f(x,y)=x^2+y^2 subject to y=x^2. Find the constrained critical points by reducing to one variable. Then explain why the ordinary condition \nabla f=\lambda\nabla g must be used with care at singular constraints.

Let f(x,y)=x on the line segment -1\le x\le1, \qquad y=0. Explain why the Lagrange multiplier equation for the smooth line y=0 finds no candidate, even though the function has a maximum and a minimum on the segment.
Let f(x,y)=|x|+y^2. Does f have a local minimum at the origin? Is the gradient defined there?
Let f(x,y)=x^2-y^2+x^4. Classify the origin.
Let f(x,y)=x^4+y^4-4xy. Find all critical points. Use the Hessian where possible.
Let f(x,y)=e^{x+y}(x^2+y^2). Show that the origin is a critical point and classify it.
Let f(x,y)=x^2+2y^2+2xy-6x. Complete the square to classify its critical point. Then check your answer with the Hessian.
Give an example of a critical point where the Hessian is the zero matrix but the point is a local minimum.
Give an example of a critical point where the Hessian is the zero matrix but the point is a saddle point.

subsection.title: Discovery project: least squares and the geometry of best fit
subsection.p: A line rarely passes exactly through experimental data. Suppose a lab records four points: (0,1),\qquad (1,2),\qquad (2,2),\qquad (3,4). We want a line y=mx+b that fits them as well as possible.

For each data point (x_i,y_i), the vertical error is mx_i+b-y_i. Instead of minimizing the sum of errors, which can cancel positive and negative mistakes, minimize the sum of squared errors: \boxed{E(m,b)=\sum_{i=1}^4(mx_i+b-y_i)^2.} For these data, \boxed{E(m,b) = (b-1)^2+(m+b-2)^2+(2m+b-2)^2+(3m+b-4)^2.}
The best-fit line is found by minimizing E. This is an optimization problem in the two variables m \qquad\text{and}\qquad b.

subsubsection.title: Part 1. The normal equations
subsubsection.p: Differentiate: E_m=2\sum_{i=1}^4 x_i(mx_i+b-y_i), and E_b=2\sum_{i=1}^4 (mx_i+b-y_i). At the minimum, E_m=0, \qquad E_b=0. These are called the normal equations.

subsubsection.title: Part 2. Why this critical point is a minimum
subsubsection.p:
The Hessian of E is
H_E = 2 \begin{pmatrix} \sum x_i^2 \amp \sum x_i\\ \sum x_i \amp 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 28 \amp 12\\ 12 \amp 8 \end{pmatrix}.
Compute \Delta=28\cdot8-12^2=224-144=80\gt 0. Since A=28\gt 0, the Hessian is positive definite. Therefore the critical point is a local minimum.
Because E(m,b) is a quadratic function whose leading quadratic part is positive definite, this local minimum is the absolute minimum.

subsubsection.title: Part 3. The geometry of the residual
subsubsection.p: Let \mathbf y=(1,2,2,4) be the vector of measured y-values. Let \mathbf 1=(1,1,1,1), \qquad \mathbf x=(0,1,2,3). The predicted values from the line y=mx+b form the vector \widehat{\mathbf y}=m\mathbf x+b\mathbf 1. The residual vector is \mathbf r=\widehat{\mathbf y}-\mathbf y.

At the best-fit line, m=b=\frac9{10}. So \widehat{\mathbf y} = \left(\frac9{10},\frac{18}{10},\frac{27}{10},\frac{36}{10}\right), and \mathbf r = \left(-\frac1{10},-\frac2{10},\frac7{10},-\frac4{10}\right). Check that \boxed{\mathbf r\cdot \mathbf 1=0} and \boxed{\mathbf r\cdot \mathbf x=0.}

The residual is perpendicular to every change caused by adjusting b or adjusting m. This is the geometric meaning of the normal equations: the error left over is orthogonal to the space of possible predictions.

subsubsection.title: What to hand in
subsubsection.p: Write a short report with the following pieces.

li.p: A plot of the four data points and the best-fit line.
The two normal equations E_m=0,\qquad E_b=0.
The Hessian calculation showing that this point gives a minimum.
The residual vector and the two dot products \mathbf r\cdot\mathbf 1=0, \qquad \mathbf r\cdot\mathbf x=0.
A paragraph explaining why best fit means a perpendicular residual.

subsection.title: Discovery project: designing a container under constraints
subsection.p: A rectangular open-top container has base side lengths x,\qquad y and height z. It must have volume 32 cubic units: xyz=32. The amount of material needed is A(x,y,z)=xy+2xz+2yz. The term xy is the base. The terms 2xz and 2yz are the four side walls. There is no top.
Find the dimensions that use the least material.

subsubsection.title: Part 1. Set up the Lagrange equations
subsubsection.p:
Let g(x,y,z)=xyz. The constraint is g=32. The gradients are 
abla A=(y+2z,\ x+2z,\ 2x+2y), and 
abla g=(yz,\ xz,\ xy). The Lagrange multiplier equation is 
abla A=\lambda
abla g. So y+2z=\lambda yz, x+2z=\lambda xz, 2x+2y=\lambda xy, together with xyz=32.

subsubsection.title: Part 2. Show that the base is square
subsubsection.p: Multiply the first equation by x: xy+2xz=\lambda xyz. Multiply the second equation by y: xy+2yz=\lambda xyz. Subtract: 2xz-2yz=0. Since z\gt 0, we get \boxed{x=y.}

Set x=y=a. The third Lagrange equation becomes 4a=\lambda a^2, so \lambda=\frac4a. The first equation becomes a+2z=\lambda az. Substitute \lambda=\frac4a: a+2z=4z. Therefore \boxed{a=2z.} So z=\frac a2.
Use the volume constraint: a^2z=32. Since z=\frac a2, we get a^2\cdot\frac a2=32, so a^3=64. Thus a=4, and z=2. The candidate is \boxed{x=4,\qquad y=4,\qquad z=2.}

subsubsection.title: Part 3. Check the minimum another way
subsubsection.p: The answer is not a cube. Since the container has no top, it is cheaper to make the base wider and the height shorter.

subsubsection.title: What to hand in
subsubsection.p: Write a short report with the following pieces.

li.p: A diagram of the open-top box with side lengths x, y, and z.
A sentence explaining why the answer is shorter than a cube.

conclusion.p: This chapter asked where a function is largest, smallest, or level to first order. That is a pointwise question. We stood on a hill and asked for the summit. We walked around a constraint and asked where the allowed motion stopped improving the value. The tools were local: df, \qquad \nabla f, \qquad H_f.

But sometimes the most interesting question is not about one point. A farmer does not only ask where the field is tallest; she asks how much crop the whole field will produce. An engineer does not only ask where a plate is hottest; she asks how much heat energy is spread across the plate. A hill is not only its highest point; it also has a total volume of earth under it.

To answer questions like that, we stop searching for a best point and start adding tiny pieces over a region. The single-variable integral added pieces along an interval. The next chapter lets the pieces fill a rectangle, a disk, or a curved region in the plane.
